Article.ArticleTitle: Corneal Densitometry for Early and Long-Term Evaluation of Surgical Outcomes After Phototherapeutic Keratectomy for Granular, Macular, and Lattice Corneal Dystrophy.

Abstract.AbstractText: To evaluate early and long-term outcomes of phototherapeutic keratectomy (PTK) in granular (GCD), macular (MCD), and lattice corneal dystrophy (LCD) using corneal densitometry as an objective marker for surgical efficacy.

In this retrospective study, 51 eyes from 31 patients (GCD: n = 27; MCD: n = 13; LCD: n = 11) undergoing PTK were analyzed. Corneal tomography and densitometry were assessed preoperatively and at intervals up to over 5 years. Mixed-model analysis was used for statistical evaluation (P ≤ 0.05).

In GCD, best-corrected visual acuity (BCVA) improved from 0.4 ± 0.3 to 0.2 ± 0.1 logMAR after 6 to 12 months, with sustained reductions in anterior densitometry (0-2 mm and 2-6 mm zones) for up to 1 year. LCD eyes showed a short-term densitometry decrease in the 2 to 6 mm anterior zone at 6 weeks, but no significant BCVA improvement. MCD eyes demonstrated improved BCVA at 6 to 12 months, despite increased densitometry in anterior and central layers at 6 weeks. After 2 years, densitometry of the anterior and central layer as well as the total corneal thickness in all diameters were not statistically different to the preoperative values in all 3 groups.

PTK effectively improves BCVA and corneal transparency in GCD, where densitometry provides a useful objective measure. In LCD, densitometry changes are transient and do not clearly reflect functional benefit. In MCD, densitometry has limited value due to diffuse stromal involvement and does not correlate with visual improvement. Overall, corneal densitometry may support preoperative planning and postoperative follow-up in selected dystrophies, with its strongest applicability in GCD.

MedlineCitation.CoiStatement: The authors have no funding or conflicts of interest to disclose.
